articles.title: Digital access to the Soviet Extraordinary State Commission: archives of war
articles.abstract: Abstract The Covid-19 pandemic and then the war in Ukraine have disrupted access to archives, intensifying scholars’ concerns about a return to Cold War conditions for research. These concerns are especially pronounced for study of the Holocaust in the Soviet Union, a field where specialists have often struggled to locate and assimilate information even before recent crises. This article examines digital options for accessing the records of the Extraordinary State Commission (Chrezvychainaia gosudarstvennaia komissiia, ChGK), established by Stalin's government on November 2, 1942, to gather evidence of Axis war crimes. Through an analysis of the digital projects launched over the past two decades, this article demonstrates that enhanced online access to Soviet documentation of Nazi atrocities has gone hand in hand with the radicalization of Putin's regime. The politicized rhetoric that frequently accompanies these undertakings has obscured the value of the digital resources themselves. Scholars of the Shoah should approach the ChGK's records as products of mass mobilization rather than uniform pronouncements from a “totalitarian” state. Equipped with this knowledge, together with the scale and comparative reach of digital methods, researchers can deepen understanding of the Holocaust against the backdrop of a new generation of war crimes in the former USSR.

articles.title: Never Again: Germans and Genocide After the Holocaust Andrew I. Port
The madwoman in the deportation train: remembering and narrating individual and collective memory in Night, Fatelessness, and Still Alive
articles.abstract: Abstract This article draws attention to the pervasive role of female madness in literary representations of Holocaust deportation. The analysis focuses on three key texts: Elie Wiesel's Night (1956), Imre Kertész's Fatelessness (1975), and Ruth Klüger's Still Alive (1992), all of which depict an elderly woman's vocal and physical expression of hopelessness and despair en route to Auschwitz. By drawing on cultural frameworks of lamentation and madness, the article demonstrates her function as a narrative double, a character whose madness reflects and amplifies the psychological turmoil of the broader prisoner community. The article emphasizes that although the madwoman appears in each of these fictional representations, she is not treated as a monolith. Instead, each survivor writer and their narrators employ her differently to either foreshadow impending horrors, to create narrative distance, or to highlight the incapacity to engage with the suffering of others. The close reading analysis offers insights into the ways in which fiction can mediate and challenge our understanding of narratives of deportation experiences and thus contributes to the broader discourse on literary representation and Holocaust memory.

articles.title: Jewishness and beyond: Jewish conversions in Hungary, 1825–1914,
FROM FRINGE TO CORE: THE TEMPLE MOUNT IN RELIGIOUS ZIONISM’S EVOLVING IDENTITY
articles.abstract: Abstract This article analyzes the transformation of the Temple Mount from a marginal symbol to a central component of Religious Zionism’s contemporary identity. Drawing on historical analysis, ideological discourse, rabbinic rulings, and empirical data on ascents to the Mount since the early 2000s, the study argues that recent developments do not represent a process of radicalization, but rather one of institutionalization and normalization within mainstream Religious Zionism. Historically, Jewish attitudes toward the Temple Mount were characterized by spiritual longing coupled with political and halachic restraint, both during the British Mandate and in the decades following the Six-Day War. Even the messianic currents of Religious Zionism initially prioritized settlement and territorial expansion over engagement with the Mount. This article traces the gradual erosion of that restraint, highlighting the role of educational frameworks, rabbinic leadership, and shifting political realities in redefining ascent to the Temple Mount as a legitimate, and even normative, religious practice. Contrary to earlier scholarship that framed Temple Mount activism primarily as the domain of extremist or subversive groups, this study demonstrates how a broad coalition of Religious Zionist rabbis and institutions reframed ascent as a measured expression of sovereignty, worship, and redemptive participation. The article concludes that the Temple Mount has emerged as a new focal point through which Religious Zionism negotiates authority, messianism, and statehood, with far-reaching implications for Israeli religious and political discourse.

articles.title: WHO’S AFRAID OF THE WORLD TO COME? THE CURIOUS CASE OF THE DISAPPEARANCE OF OTHERWORLDLINESS IN MODERN ORTHODOX JEWISH THEOLOGY
articles.abstract: Abstract This article examines the conspicuous absence of otherworldliness in modern Orthodox Jewish theology, particularly in the works of Rabbi Joseph B. Soloveitchik and his intellectual successors, Irving Greenberg, Jonathan Sacks, and David Hartman, contrasting this with its persistence in Haredi theology. While traditional Jewish thought emphasized the world to come as a primary motivation for observance, modern Orthodox theology, as shaped by Soloveitchik’s Halakhic Man, prioritizes this-worldly sanctification, historical redemption, and covenantal responsibility. This shift is attributed not solely to engagement with modernity (as Haredi thinkers also navigated secular contexts) but to intellectual, cultural, and pastoral factors. These include Soloveitchik’s theological framework, the modern Orthodox community’s integration into secular society, and a pastoral sensitivity to audiences more responsive to immediate, empirical benefits of Jewish practice. The article argues that this reorientation reflects a strategic adaptation to affirm the dignity of the present world, offering a vision of Judaism that reconciles tradition with contemporary life.

articles.title: THE POSTWAR BATTLE FOR THE SOULS OF INDIAN JEWS
articles.abstract: Abstract This article explores the forgotten postwar battle between the leaders of the Orthodox and Conservative movements of American Judaism for the affiliation of the Bene Israel Jews of India, as both movements tried to grow their international profiles. An envisioned Bene Israel alliance with the American Conservative movement upset leaders of Orthodox Judaism, who formed a counterfederation. Yet, the situation in India was more complex than either denomination admitted. What in America passed for religious division between Jews was in India also a matter of race and class, even caste. Many Bene Israel defined themselves in opposition to the Orthodoxy of Baghdadi Jews, established merchants in India who largely denied the religious authenticity of the more impoverished Bene Israel, and blocked their full access to Baghdadi religious institutions. This produced the religious void that American Orthodox and Conservative leaders aimed to fill. Soon, however, most of the Bene Israel had immigrated to Israel, where the Sephardic Chief Rabbi refused to certify their collective Jewish status, adding a fascinating twist to the story.

articles.title: IDEOLOGICALLY JUSTIFIED VIOLENCE AMONG ISRAEL’S HAREDI SOCIETY
articles.abstract: Abstract In recent decades, the Israeli press occasionally reports on various sorts of violent acts taken by Haredi Jews and aimed at other Haredi individuals or groups. These acts range from slander, discrimination, threatening, and banning, to damaging private property, stoning, and even physical violence. Unlike regular crimes, the perpetrators of these acts of violence justify their actions for ideological and religious reasons, insisting they are only doing what is right in God’s or their rabbi’s eyes. This article reviews the history of ideologically justified violence in Jewish history in general and in Eretz Israel in particular. This review reveals that the rising number of such incidents, as well as their spreading to more Haredi groups, indicates that in Israel, particularly since the late twentieth century, Haredi society has adopted ideologically justified violence as a legitimate tool to enforce its social and religious norms.

articles.title: SARTRE’S “JEWISH QUESTION” AND A PLAUSIBLE DESCRIPTION OF THE ANTISEMITIC
articles.abstract: Abstract Sartre’s essay “On the Jewish Question” was written between 1944 and 1945 and published in 1946. It is a remarkable essay, surprising both for its intelligent commentary and for the many insights it reveals. It is written in extraordinary prose, reminiscent of a world in which the subtlety and complexity of good speech and good writing were encouraged and appreciated. The essay is full of pertinent observations and intelligent and witty analysis. Some, but not all, of these will be considered in the present essay. Thus, as well as analyzing the weaknesses in Sartre’s argument, the emphasis will be on the redeeming aspects of his essay and how these features can help us understand the present situation that has suddenly become stubbornly anti-Semitic.

articles.title: Through a Distorted Lens: Belonging and Ethical Representation of Jewish Identity and Zionism in Selected Palestinian Advocacy Picture Books
